title: Chapter 5: Projectile Motion - Study Notes
source: A Quick Review of E-PCM for Engineering Entrance - Physics

sections.heading: Basic Properties of Projectile Motion
sections.content: In projectile motion, acceleration is due to gravity only. The path (trajectory) of the projectile is parabolic. The horizontal component of velocity remains constant throughout the motion. Velocity is minimum at the highest point.
formulas: Range, R = u² sin2θ / g
Max Height, H = u² sin²θ / 2g
Time of flight, T_f = 2u sinθ / g
Time of ascent = Time of descent = t_a = t_d = (1/2)T_f = u sinθ / g
H/R = tanθ / 4
For maximum range, θ = 45°, R_max = u²/g, H = R_max/4
Equation of trajectory: y = x tanθ - gx² / (2u² cos²θ)
y = ax - bx² where R = a/b, H = a²/4b
In terms of range and max height: y = tanθ · x - (tanθ/R) x²  or  y = tanθ · x - (tan²θ / 4H) x²
specialCases: At highest point: angle between velocity and acceleration is 90°.
KE is minimum at highest point.
A person who can throw max horizontal distance R₀ can throw max height R₀/2.
Average velocity during time of ascent: V_avg = (u/2)√(1 + 3cos²θ)
Average velocity when projectile strikes ground: V_avg = u cosθ

sections.heading: Kinetic and Potential Energy in Projectile Motion
sections.content: If a projectile is projected with kinetic energy K at angle θ, the KE and PE change along the trajectory.
formulas: KE at highest point (Min KE): K_H = K cos²θ
PE at highest point (Max PE): U_H = K sin²θ
Min KE = K cos²θ = P² cos²θ / (2m) where P = initial momentum
specialCases: If projected with momentum P: change in momentum at any time Δp = mgt.
At highest point (t = u sinθ/g): Δp = p sinθ.
At striking point (t = 2u sinθ/g): Δp = 2p sinθ.
If θ = 30°: Δp at striking point = p (100% of p).

sections.heading: Speed and Velocity Relations
sections.content: Speed and velocity of the projectile at different points and times.
formulas: If projectile makes angle α after time t: speed v = u cosθ / cosα = u cosθ · secα
Velocity components at time t: v_x = u cosθ, v_y = u sinθ - gt
Speed at time t: v = √(v_x² + v_y²) = √(u² - 2u sinθ · gt + g²t²)
Angle with horizontal at time t: tanα = (u sinθ - gt) / (u cosθ) = v_y / v_x
If velocity is perpendicular to initial velocity at time t: v = u cotθ, t = (u/g) cosecθ
Change in speed at highest point: Δ|v| = u(1 - cosθ) = 2u sin²(θ/2)

sections.heading: Same Range — Two Angles of Projection
sections.content: For a given speed, the same range is obtained for two complementary angles θ and (90°-θ).
formulas: θ + α = 90° for same range
Ratio of max heights: H_θ/H_α = tan²θ = cot²α
Ratio of times of flight: (t_f)_θ / (t_f)_α = tanθ or cotα
R = 4√(H_θ × H_α)  ⟹  H₁H₂ ∝ R²
H₁ + H₂ = u²/2g
specialCases: For same range R at angles θ and (90°-θ): t₁t₂ = 2R/g ∝ R.
Maximum range R_max = u²/g is achieved at θ = 45°.

sections.heading: Horizontal Projection from a Height
sections.content: A body projected horizontally from height H with velocity u.
formulas: Time of flight: t_f = √(2H/g)
Speed after time t: v = √(u² + g²t²)
Angle with horizontal after time t: tanα = gt/u
Horizontal range: R = u × √(2H/g)
nth step hit by ball rolled off staircase (width b, height h): n = 2hu²/(gb²)
specialCases: If two bodies are projected horizontally from height with velocities u₁ and u₂ in opposite directions, their velocities are perpendicular at t = √(u₁u₂)/g.
v₁ = √(u₁² + u₁u₂), v₂ = √(u₂² + u₁u₂) when velocities are perpendicular.
Their position vectors are perpendicular at t = 2√(u₁u₂)/g.
A man must aim higher than target to hit it.
If a man fires directly at a monkey that starts falling simultaneously, the bullet hits the monkey regardless of angle.

sections.heading: Ball Dropped on Inclined Plane
sections.content: If a ball is dropped from height h from the top of an inclined plane of inclination α, it elastically collides and strikes the inclined plane again at:
formulas: Distance along incline: S = 8h sinα
